knowledge.title: curl Proxy-Authorization leak on proxy-to-direct redirect transition
knowledge.knowledge: In curl (at least through version 8.17.0), the Proxy-Authorization header is not stripped when following a redirect that transitions the request from a proxied connection to a direct connection. This occurs when the redirect target matches a --noproxy rule or otherwise bypasses the configured proxy. The header, which is semantically hop-by-hop and intended only for the proxy, is sent verbatim to the origin server on the redirected request.
trigger_condition: Encountering a curl-based client or automation that uses an HTTP proxy with authentication and follows redirects (-L)
Observing that the redirect target resolves to a host that matches --noproxy patterns or falls outside proxy scope
Confirming that Proxy-Authorization is set via -H flag or proxy credential configuration

general: HTTP client libraries
CLI HTTP tools
proxy-aware HTTP clients
CI/CD pipeline HTTP requests

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When an HTTP client follows a redirect that changes the connection context (e.g., proxied to direct, or TLS to plain), hop-by-hop headers that were appropriate for the original connection context may be incorrectly carried over to the new connection context. The redirect acts as a context boundary that the client fails to enforce.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [HTTP client follows redirects through a proxy] AND [redirect target bypasses the proxy (noproxy match or different routing)] THEN [hop-by-hop headers like Proxy-Authorization may leak to the origin server]
generalizes_to: hop-by-hop header leakage across connection context changes
credential leakage via redirect-induced context transitions
HTTP client header sanitization failures on redirect chains

pitfalls: Assuming that only proxy-auth configured via --proxy-user is affected — manually set -H 'Proxy-Authorization: ...' headers exhibit the same behavior
Testing only same-proxy redirects and missing the proxy-to-direct transition case, which is the specific trigger
Overlooking this in environments where --noproxy includes localhost/internal ranges, which is common and makes the redirect target likely to match
knowledge.chain_potential: An attacker who controls or can influence a proxy response (e.g., via MITM on unencrypted proxy connection, or a malicious proxy) can issue a 302 redirect to an attacker-controlled server. The victim's curl client will follow the redirect and send the Proxy-Authorization credentials directly to the attacker's server, enabling proxy credential theft and subsequent unauthorized proxy access.
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Demonstrated with a concrete reproducible PoC against curl 8.17.0, the behavior is deterministic and tied to curl's redirect-following logic not re-evaluating header appropriateness after connection context changes.

knowledge.title: Proxy-to-direct redirect as credential harvesting vector
knowledge.knowledge: When an HTTP client is configured with both a proxy and a noproxy list that includes internal/localhost addresses, a redirect from the proxied request to a noproxy-matching address creates an implicit trust boundary crossing. The presence of --noproxy 127.0.0.1 or --noproxy localhost in curl configurations is a signal that redirect-based credential harvesting is possible if the attacker can influence 3xx responses from the proxy or proxied target.
trigger_condition: Observing curl invocations or libcurl configurations that combine proxy settings with --noproxy rules covering internal addresses
Discovering that the proxy endpoint or proxied target is attacker-influenceable (e.g., open proxy, SSRF, or MITM position on HTTP proxy connection)

general: HTTP clients with proxy bypass lists
enterprise proxy configurations
container/CI environments with proxy settings

abstracted_pattern.narrative: A proxy bypass list (noproxy) that includes internal addresses creates a two-zone model where different security contexts apply. Any mechanism that causes a request to cross from the proxied zone to the noproxy zone during a single request lifecycle (such as a redirect) can carry context-specific credentials across the boundary.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [client has proxy + noproxy configuration] AND [attacker can trigger redirect from proxied target to noproxy-matching host] THEN [proxy-scoped credentials leak to the redirect target]
generalizes_to: trust boundary violations via redirect chains
credential scope violations in multi-zone network configurations
header leakage across security context transitions

pitfalls: Focusing only on the direct client-side curl usage and missing that many higher-level libraries and tools use libcurl internally, inheriting this behavior
Assuming HTTPS proxy connections are safe — the redirect itself can come from the proxied HTTP target, not just the proxy
knowledge.chain_potential: Combine with SSRF or open redirect on any proxied target to redirect the request to an attacker-controlled server matching noproxy rules, harvesting proxy credentials without needing proxy-level access.
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The attack chain is logical and the curl behavior is verified, but real-world exploitability depends on the attacker's ability to influence redirect responses, which varies by deployment.

knowledge.title: Tiered URL decode rejection flags leave CRLF gaps
knowledge.knowledge: URL decoding functions that offer configurable rejection levels (e.g., reject-null vs reject-all-control-chars) often default to or are called with the minimal rejection tier. When a protocol handler uses the null-byte-only rejection mode, CR/LF bytes survive decoding and reach the wire verbatim. This gap is non-obvious because the presence of *any* rejection flag suggests the developer considered injection, creating a false sense of completeness.
trigger_condition: Observing a URL decoding call site that passes a rejection/filter flag rather than doing blanket sanitization
The rejection flag specifically targets null bytes or a narrow subset of control characters
The decoded output is sent over a text-based protocol where line breaks are command delimiters (Gopher, SMTP, Redis RESP, Memcached text protocol)

general: HTTP clients with multi-protocol support
Libraries with configurable input validation strictness
SSRF-capable proxy components

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a decoding or sanitization function offers multiple strictness levels and a caller chooses a lower tier, the gap between what is blocked and what is dangerous for the specific downstream consumer creates an injection surface. The existence of the filtering mechanism masks the inadequacy of the chosen level.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [URL/input decoding uses a tiered rejection mode] AND [the selected tier blocks only a subset of dangerous characters] AND [downstream protocol uses unblocked characters as command delimiters] THEN [injection through the unblocked character class is possible despite apparent sanitization]
generalizes_to: partial input validation in any multi-protocol client
configurable sanitization where callers choose insufficient strictness
protocol-specific injection through characters not covered by generic filters

pitfalls: Assuming that the presence of any rejection flag means all dangerous characters are handled - must verify which specific characters the flag covers
Focusing only on HTTP-relevant injection (header injection) and missing that other supported protocols like Gopher transmit decoded paths with no framing
knowledge.chain_potential: Gopher CRLF injection enables SSRF command injection into line-oriented internal services (Redis, Memcached, SMTP) - the CRLF becomes full command injection in the downstream protocol
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Verified in curl source code with specific flag constants; the REJECT_ZERO vs REJECT_CTRL distinction is concrete and reproducible

knowledge.title: location.pathname double-slash protocol-relative URL hijack in AJAX
knowledge.knowledge: When JavaScript uses location.pathname to construct AJAX request URLs (e.g., `var url = prefix + '/endpoint'` where `prefix = location.pathname`), a path starting with `//attacker.com` is treated as a protocol-relative URL by the browser's HTTP stack. The web server at the target domain may serve the page normally for paths like `https://target.com//attacker.com/path`, but the subsequent AJAX call resolves the constructed URL as `//attacker.com/path/endpoint`, which the browser interprets as `https://attacker.com/path/endpoint`. This causes the AJAX response to be fetched from the attacker's server and, if the response is written to the DOM, achieves XSS under the target's origin.
trigger_condition: Observing JavaScript that reads location.pathname or location.href to construct URLs for AJAX/fetch/XMLHttpRequest calls
Discovering that the server normalizes or accepts paths with leading double slashes (e.g., https://target.com//anything returns 200 instead of 404/redirect)
Confirming the AJAX response is inserted into the DOM via innerHTML, jQuery .html(), or similar sink

general: Any web application using location.pathname for dynamic content loading URLs
JavaScript applications that construct API endpoints from browser-provided URL components

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When application code treats browser-provided URL path components as implicitly safe for constructing secondary request URLs, an attacker can exploit URL parsing ambiguity. The browser's URL parser for navigation and the URL parser for subsequent requests may interpret the same path differently - particularly when protocol-relative URL syntax (//) appears within a path component, causing requests to be redirected to attacker-controlled servers.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [client-side code uses location.pathname to build AJAX/fetch URLs] AND [server accepts paths with leading double slashes] AND [AJAX response is written to DOM] THEN [attacker can serve malicious content from external domain under target origin via //attacker.com path prefix]
generalizes_to: DOM XSS via AJAX response injection
URL parsing differential between navigation and programmatic requests
Protocol-relative URL injection in path-derived URL construction
Client-side request forgery via pathname manipulation

pitfalls: Many web servers or CDNs normalize double slashes in paths to single slashes via redirect, which blocks this attack - always verify the server preserves the double slash
Same-origin policy and CORS can block the cross-origin AJAX request - the attacker's server must set Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers appropriately
If the application uses a relative path without the leading location.pathname (e.g., just '/endpoint'), this technique does not apply
Some frameworks strip or encode the pathname before use, preventing the protocol-relative interpretation
knowledge.chain_potential: Achieving JavaScript execution under the target's origin enables session hijacking, credential theft, or pivoting to exploit authenticated API endpoints. Can also be chained with service workers registration for persistent access.
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The technique is demonstrated with a working proof-of-concept in a real bug bounty program, and the underlying browser behavior (protocol-relative URL resolution from pathname) is well-defined and consistent across browsers.

knowledge.title: location.pathname in URL construction signals AJAX request hijack potential
knowledge.knowledge: The presence of `location.pathname` or `location.href` being concatenated into URL strings for AJAX/fetch requests in JavaScript source code is a strong signal for potential request hijack vulnerabilities. Unlike query parameters or hash fragments, the pathname component is often assumed safe by developers because it appears to be server-controlled, but it is fully attacker-controllable via crafted links. This pattern is especially common in minified production JavaScript files (e.g., pdp.min.js) where the pathname is used as a prefix to construct API endpoint URLs.
trigger_condition: Observing location.pathname or window.location.pathname concatenated with strings to form URLs in JavaScript source (especially in minified bundles)
Discovering the constructed URL is passed to $.ajax, fetch(), XMLHttpRequest, or similar request APIs
Confirming the response from the request is rendered into the DOM

specific: E-commerce product detail pages (PDP) with dynamic content loading
general: Any JavaScript application constructing request URLs from location properties
SPAs with client-side routing that also make API calls based on current path

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When reviewing JavaScript for DOM XSS, developers-provided URL components (location.pathname, location.href) being used in request URL construction are often overlooked because the path feels like a server-side concern. But these values are fully attacker-controlled through crafted navigation links and represent a class of source-to-sink flows that static analyzers and manual reviewers frequently miss.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [location.pathname is used in string concatenation to build request URLs] AND [the result is passed to a network request API] THEN [the request destination is attacker-controllable, creating a client-side request forgery or XSS vector]
generalizes_to: Client-side request forgery via URL component manipulation
Taint analysis for browser location properties as XSS sources
JavaScript code review prioritization for DOM XSS

pitfalls: Grepping only for location.hash or location.search as DOM XSS sources and missing location.pathname as an equally controllable source
Assuming location.pathname is safe because it does not contain query parameters or fragments
Overlooking this pattern in minified JavaScript where variable names are obfuscated - the location.pathname reference itself is not minified
knowledge.chain_potential: Identifying this pattern is the first step; exploitation requires confirming the server accepts double-slash paths and the response sink allows script execution. Enables prioritization of which JavaScript files to audit deeply.
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The writeup demonstrates a real-world instance of this exact code pattern leading to a confirmed DOM XSS. The pattern of using location.pathname for URL construction is common in jQuery-era web applications.

knowledge.title: CLI argument parsing as incidental CRLF defense layer in libcurl
knowledge.knowledge: libcurl's CLI frontend (the curl command) performs argument parsing that incidentally strips or rejects CRLF characters from options like username, but the underlying C library API (CURLOPT_USERNAME) passes these characters through to protocol handlers without equivalent sanitization. The imap_atom function in lib/imap.c is responsible for quoting special characters in IMAP protocol fields but does not treat \r and \n as characters requiring quoting or rejection. This creates a discrepancy where the same vulnerability is unexploitable via CLI but exploitable in any application using libcurl as a library.
trigger_condition: Encountering an application that uses libcurl programmatically (not via CLI subprocess) for IMAP, SMTP, or similar line-oriented protocols
Observing that user-controlled input flows into CURLOPT_USERNAME or similar identity-setting options without application-level sanitization
Confirming that the protocol handler's quoting function (e.g., imap_atom) does not enumerate CR/LF as special characters

general: Applications embedding libcurl as a library
Line-oriented protocol clients with quoting functions
Any library where CLI wrapper provides incidental input filtering

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a security-sensitive library has both a CLI frontend and a programmatic API, the CLI's input parsing may incidentally sanitize dangerous characters that the library API passes through raw. Vulnerabilities that appear unexploitable via CLI testing may be fully exploitable in applications using the library directly. The attack surface of the library is strictly larger than the attack surface of its CLI wrapper.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [a library has a CLI frontend that incidentally sanitizes protocol-injection characters] AND [the library API does not perform equivalent sanitization] THEN [applications using the library API directly are vulnerable to protocol injection that CLI testing would miss]

pitfalls: Testing only via the CLI tool (e.g., curl command) and concluding the library is safe — the CLI's argument parsing masks library-level vulnerabilities

Assuming that because a protocol handler has a quoting/escaping function (like imap_atom), it handles all dangerous characters — the function may only quote a subset of special characters relevant to the protocol grammar but miss injection-relevant characters like CR/LF

Overlooking that the PoC result may show collapsed output (CRLF rendered on same line in terminal) rather than actual multi-line injection — always verify with hex dump or raw packet capture to confirm true CRLF transmission
knowledge.chain_potential: Combining this with an application that accepts untrusted usernames for IMAP operations (e.g., webmail, email migration tools, backup services) enables arbitrary IMAP command injection within an authenticated session context, potentially allowing mailbox deletion, creation, or data exfiltration
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The vulnerability pattern is clearly demonstrated in the code path analysis, but the PoC result as shown in the report (single-line output) does not conclusively demonstrate successful multi-line injection — the CRLF may have been collapsed rather than interpreted as a command separator. The general pattern of CLI-vs-library attack surface gaps is well-established across multiple projects.

knowledge.title: Protocol injection testing requires library-level API access not CLI
knowledge.knowledge: When auditing protocol client libraries for injection vulnerabilities in identity fields (username, password, mailbox names), testing through the library's CLI tool is insufficient because CLI argument parsing, shell escaping, and input validation layers filter characters that the library API would pass through. Direct API-level testing with programmatic harnesses (C programs, Python bindings, etc.) is necessary to reach the actual protocol serialization code path with arbitrary byte sequences including null bytes, CRLF, and other control characters.
trigger_condition: Encountering a protocol client library that also ships a CLI tool (e.g., curl/libcurl, openssl CLI/libssl)
Observing that protocol handler code has quoting or escaping functions for protocol-specific special characters
Discovering that the quoting function's character set does not include all ASCII control characters

specific: Any curl protocol handler using atom/quoting functions
general: Protocol client libraries with CLI frontends
Libraries used for server-side email operations
Any dual-interface library (CLI + API)

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When testing protocol client libraries for injection, always write minimal programmatic harnesses that call the library API directly rather than relying on the CLI tool. The CLI tool's input processing pipeline introduces filtering that does not exist in the library's API path, creating a false sense of security.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [testing a protocol library for injection via its CLI frontend] AND [the CLI performs any input parsing/validation before calling library functions] THEN [the test results underestimate the library's actual attack surface]

pitfalls: Writing PoC code that uses system() or popen() to invoke the CLI tool instead of calling the library API directly — this reintroduces the CLI's input filtering
Assuming that shell escaping is the only barrier — even programmatic language bindings may add their own sanitization layer before calling the C library

Forgetting to simulate the protocol handshake (server greeting, capability exchange) when testing with raw listeners — libcurl will not send the injection payload until the protocol state machine advances past initial handshake
knowledge.chain_potential: Identifying library-level injection that CLI testing misses enables targeting all downstream applications that embed the library, which often have much larger deployment footprints than direct CLI usage
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The principle that CLI tools add input filtering absent from their underlying libraries is well-established and has been verified across multiple library/CLI pairs (curl, openssl, wget). The specific IMAP protocol handshake requirement is directly demonstrated in the PoC steps.

knowledge.title: Self-XSS to stored XSS via write-capable IDOR
knowledge.knowledge: A self-stored XSS that only fires in the attacker's own session becomes a full stored XSS affecting arbitrary users when a write-capable IDOR exists on the same object. The IDOR's ability to edit another user's data (e.g., via PUT with an incremental ID) allows injecting the XSS payload into a victim's stored records, so the payload executes in the victim's browser context when they interact with that record (e.g., viewing or deleting it).
trigger_condition: Discovering a stored XSS that only triggers in the submitter's own session (self-XSS)
Observing that the stored object (e.g., supplier record) is editable via API with an object ID parameter
Confirming that object IDs are sequential/predictable and the edit endpoint lacks proper authorization checks

general: Multi-user web applications with per-user stored records
Applications with CRUD APIs using direct object references

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a stored XSS is dismissed as self-only because the injected content is scoped to the submitting user's data, a write-capable authorization bypass on the same data object converts it into a cross-user stored XSS. The XSS payload is written into another user's record via the authorization flaw, and executes when that user interacts with their own data.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [self-stored XSS exists on a user-owned object] AND [write IDOR exists on the same object type] THEN [the self-XSS becomes a stored XSS exploitable against any user whose object ID is known]
generalizes_to: combining low-severity injection with authorization flaws for cross-user impact
stored payload delivery via IDOR write primitives

pitfalls: Dismissing self-XSS as unexploitable without checking for write-capable IDORs on the same object — many bug bounty programs reject self-XSS alone, but the chain makes it critical
Assuming that previous IDOR fixes cover all HTTP methods — read IDORs may be fixed while PUT/PATCH/DELETE remain vulnerable
The XSS may only trigger on a specific user action (e.g., delete confirmation), making reproduction timing-dependent and requiring social engineering awareness for impact assessment
knowledge.chain_potential: The stored XSS executing in victim context enables session hijacking, account takeover, or further CSRF-based actions within the application
knowledge.confidence_rationale: This is a well-documented and repeatedly verified chain pattern in bug bounty programs; the writeup demonstrates a concrete real-world instance with payout

knowledge.title: Partial IDOR fix signals remaining write-method IDOR
knowledge.knowledge: When an application has recently patched IDOR vulnerabilities on read operations (GET/view), the corresponding write operations (PUT/PATCH/DELETE) on the same or related endpoints frequently remain vulnerable. Fixes applied under time pressure to 'sitewide' IDORs often address the most visible access patterns (viewing data) while missing mutation endpoints that use different controller actions or middleware paths.
trigger_condition: Observing that an application recently fixed reported IDOR vulnerabilities on read/view endpoints
Discovering that the application uses incremental integer IDs for object references in API requests
Finding that PUT/PATCH requests to the same resource accept arbitrary object IDs without returning authorization errors

specific: Web applications with RESTful CRUD APIs
general: Applications that have undergone partial IDOR remediation
APIs where authorization is implemented per-endpoint rather than via middleware

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Authorization fixes applied to one HTTP method on a resource endpoint often do not propagate to other methods on the same resource. When read-IDORs are fixed, write-IDORs on the same objects are a high-probability finding because many frameworks route different HTTP methods through different controller actions, each requiring independent authorization checks.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [IDOR was recently fixed on read/view operations] AND [the API uses RESTful patterns with per-action authorization] THEN [write operations (PUT/PATCH/DELETE) on the same resource likely remain vulnerable to IDOR]
generalizes_to: per-method authorization gaps in REST APIs
regression testing after access control fixes

pitfalls: Assuming a 'sitewide IDOR fix' actually covers all HTTP methods and all endpoints — the fix scope is often narrower than described
Only retesting the exact same endpoints that were reported, rather than testing related mutation endpoints on the same resource
knowledge.chain_potential: Write-capable IDORs enable data modification attacks and can escalate otherwise unexploitable injection flaws (self-XSS, self-CSRF) into cross-user attacks
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Based on a single writeup instance, but the pattern of incomplete IDOR fixes is widely corroborated across bug bounty literature

knowledge.title: Prioritize deletion UI paths for XSS trigger discovery
knowledge.knowledge: Stored XSS payloads in record name/title fields that do not fire during normal display or editing often trigger during deletion confirmation flows. Delete confirmation dialogs frequently render the object name in a different template context (modal, popup, inline confirmation) that lacks the output encoding applied to the main list/detail views.
trigger_condition: Observing that an XSS payload stored in a name/title field does not execute when viewing or editing the record
Discovering that the application has delete functionality with confirmation dialogs for the same records
Noticing that the confirmation dialog renders the record name dynamically (e.g., 'Are you sure you want to delete <name>?')

specific: Web applications with CRUD interfaces for named records
general: Applications with confirmation dialogs that reflect stored data
Admin panels and management interfaces with delete functionality

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Secondary rendering paths for stored data (deletion confirmations, export previews, email notifications, error messages) often have weaker output encoding than primary display paths. When an injection payload does not fire in the main view, alternative rendering contexts for the same data field are high-value targets for execution.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [stored XSS payload in a name field does not trigger in primary display] AND [the application renders that field in secondary UI paths like delete confirmation] THEN [the secondary rendering path likely lacks equivalent output encoding and will execute the payload]
generalizes_to: inconsistent output encoding across UI contexts
stored XSS in non-primary display flows

pitfalls: Only testing XSS execution in the primary list/detail view and concluding the field is properly sanitized
Missing that the delete confirmation might be rendered server-side in some cases and client-side in others, with different encoding behavior
knowledge.chain_potential: Identifying the trigger path is essential for demonstrating exploitability — a stored XSS that only fires on delete requires either social engineering the victim to delete the record or chaining with a CSRF to trigger deletion
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The specific observation about delete dialogs as XSS trigger paths is documented in this report and consistent with general knowledge about inconsistent encoding, but limited to one concrete instance

knowledge.title: Error/edge-case pages bypass input validation enforced on main flows
knowledge.knowledge: In large web applications with a shared parameter (like 'continue' or 'redirect') used across many pages, error-state or edge-case pages (such as 'service not allowed', 'account disabled', or 'access denied' interstitials) frequently lack the same input validation applied to the parameter on primary login/redirect flows. These conditional pages are often developed by different teams or added later and miss security controls that the main flow enforces.
trigger_condition: Encountering a well-validated redirect/continue parameter across primary authentication or navigation flows in a large application
Observing error pages, interstitial pages, or edge-case flows that also accept the same redirect parameter
Discovering that the application requires a specific state to reach these pages (e.g., multi-account login, misconfigured service, expired session)

general: Large SaaS platforms with multi-tenant admin consoles
Applications with shared redirect parameters across many endpoints
OAuth/SSO login flows with multiple account states

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a redirect or continuation parameter is used across dozens of endpoints in a large application, the validation is typically enforced at the main flow level. Edge-case pages — error handlers, service interstitials, account-switching pages — are often built separately and may not inherit the same validation logic. Testing these less-traveled paths with the same injection payloads that fail on the main flow can reveal bypasses.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF a redirect/continue parameter is validated on primary flows AND the application has error/interstitial pages accepting the same parameter THEN those edge-case pages are likely to have weaker or missing validation of that parameter
generalizes_to: redirect parameter validation bypass on error pages
input validation inconsistency across application states
security control gaps in conditional/edge-case UI flows

pitfalls: Assuming that if the main login flow validates the parameter, all pages do — validation is often per-handler, not global middleware
Only testing the parameter on pages that are easily reachable — the vulnerable pages may require specific account states (multi-login, specific domain configs) to even render
Missing the vulnerable page entirely because it only appears under compound conditions (e.g., logged into multiple accounts AND accessing an unconfigured service)
knowledge.chain_potential: XSS on an admin console domain enables full account takeover chains: creating super-admin users, disabling 2FA, resetting passwords, and modifying DNS/mail routing settings
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Verified in Google's admin console, and the pattern of inconsistent validation across page states is a recurring finding in large web applications with many endpoints sharing parameters

knowledge.title: Redirect parameter to DOM XSS via form value population
knowledge.knowledge: When a URL parameter is not directly used in a redirect but instead populated into HTML form element values (such as radio button values) that are later read by JavaScript and assigned to window.location.href, the injection vector is indirect. Standard redirect-parameter validation often checks the server-side redirect but misses the client-side path where the parameter value flows into DOM element attributes first and then into navigation. The javascript: URI scheme is executable when assigned to window.location.href, turning what appears to be a URL parameter into a DOM XSS vector.
trigger_condition: Observing a URL parameter value reflected into form element attributes (radio buttons, hidden inputs, select options) rather than directly into a redirect
Discovering JavaScript that reads form element values and assigns them to window.location.href or window.location
Finding that server-side redirect validation is absent because the server treats the value as a form pre-fill rather than a redirect target

specific: Google Apps Admin Console ServiceNotAllowed page
general: Account-switching interfaces
Multi-step form flows with URL-driven pre-population
Any page where URL parameters populate form values consumed by client-side navigation

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Server-side validation of redirect parameters often only covers direct server-side redirects (302 responses). When the same parameter is instead embedded into HTML form element values and later consumed by client-side JavaScript for navigation, the server may not apply redirect validation because from the server's perspective the parameter is just populating a form field, not performing a redirect. The actual redirect happens entirely client-side.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF a URL parameter populates a form element value AND client-side JavaScript reads that value for window.location assignment THEN server-side redirect validation is likely bypassed because the server does not recognize the value as a redirect target
generalizes_to: server-side vs client-side redirect validation gaps
indirect DOM XSS through form value population
javascript: URI injection via non-redirect parameter paths

pitfalls: Only testing for direct redirects (302/meta refresh) and missing the client-side navigation path through form values
Dismissing a parameter as non-exploitable because the server doesn't perform a redirect, when the client-side code does
Failing to trace the full data flow from URL parameter -> HTML attribute -> JavaScript read -> navigation assignment
knowledge.chain_potential: DOM XSS on admin domains enables same-origin JavaScript execution, allowing CSRF-free API calls to perform any admin action including user creation, permission changes, and security setting modifications
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Confirmed exploitation path in Google's admin console; the indirect data flow pattern (URL param -> DOM attribute -> JS navigation) is a well-established but frequently overlooked DOM XSS class

knowledge.title: Prioritize compound-state pages when testing shared parameters
knowledge.knowledge: Pages that require multiple simultaneous conditions to render (e.g., being logged into multiple accounts, having a specific service configuration, being in a particular tenant state) are significantly more likely to contain validation gaps than single-condition pages. These compound-state pages have lower test coverage both from automated scanners (which rarely achieve the required state) and from manual testers (who may not explore all state combinations). The ServiceNotAllowed page in Google's admin required being logged into at least two accounts AND accessing an unconfigured service — this compound requirement meant it received less security scrutiny.
trigger_condition: Encountering an application with multi-account or multi-tenant functionality where different states produce different page variants
Observing that certain pages only appear under specific account/configuration combinations
Discovering that a shared parameter (redirect, continue, callback) appears on these hard-to-reach pages

general: Multi-tenant SaaS platforms
Applications with multi-account login support
Enterprise admin consoles with service/feature toggles

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Security testing effort follows accessibility: easily reachable pages get the most scrutiny while pages requiring compound conditions to render get the least. When a security-critical parameter is shared across both well-tested and hard-to-reach pages, the hard-to-reach pages are the highest-value targets because they're most likely to have validation gaps.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF a security-critical parameter exists on pages requiring compound state conditions to render THEN those pages are disproportionately likely to have weaker validation compared to easily-reachable pages using the same parameter
generalizes_to: validation coverage gaps in hard-to-reach application states
security testing of multi-condition application flows

pitfalls: Spending all testing time on easily reachable endpoints and never achieving the compound state needed to reach the vulnerable page
Not systematically mapping which pages accept shared parameters — the vulnerable endpoint may not be discoverable through normal crawling
Assuming that because the parameter is validated on the main flow, other pages must use the same validation library or middleware
knowledge.chain_potential: Finding unvalidated parameters on admin-scoped pages directly enables high-impact exploitation since the execution context already has elevated privileges
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The principle that hard-to-reach pages have less security coverage is consistently validated across bug bounty findings; this specific example confirms the pattern for Google's admin infrastructure

knowledge.title: HTML blacklist bypass via deprecated and non-standard tags
knowledge.knowledge: HTML sanitizers that use a blacklist of tag names (filtering known dangerous tags like script, iframe, img, svg) are vulnerable to bypass through deprecated or non-standard HTML tags such as <fn>, <credit>, or other obscure tags that browsers still parse as valid DOM elements capable of carrying event handler attributes. These tags are rarely included in manually-curated blacklists because they are not commonly associated with XSS vectors.
trigger_condition: Observing that common HTML tags (script, a, span, img, iframe) are stripped or filtered from user input
Discovering that the filtering appears to be blacklist-based rather than whitelist-based (some tags pass through while known-dangerous ones are removed)
Confirming that attribute-level filtering is separate from tag-level filtering (event handlers may still be allowed on tags that pass the blacklist)

general: Web applications using blacklist-based HTML tag filtering
Custom HTML sanitizers that enumerate blocked tags
Legacy web applications with hand-rolled input validation

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When an application filters HTML by maintaining a list of blocked tag names rather than allowing only known-safe tags, obscure, deprecated, or non-standard tags that browsers still render as DOM elements can bypass the filter. These elements can carry event handler attributes (onmouseover, onfocus, etc.) enabling script execution.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [HTML tags are filtered by blacklist] AND [obscure/deprecated tags are not in the blacklist] AND [event handler attributes are permitted on surviving tags] THEN [XSS is achievable via non-standard tag with event handler]
generalizes_to: any deny-list approach to content filtering

pitfalls: Using <plaintext> as the bypass tag will cause the remainder of the page to render as unformatted text, destroying page layout and making the injection immediately obvious — prefer tags like <fn> or <credit> that render invisibly
Some browsers may not recognize all non-standard tags equally — test across target browsers before relying on a specific obscure tag
Modern HTML sanitizers (DOMPurify, etc.) use whitelist approaches and will not be vulnerable to this technique
knowledge.chain_potential: Enables stored XSS which can be chained with missing CSRF protections on profile edit forms to create self-propagating XSS worms
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Demonstrated and verified in a real-world eBay bug bounty finding; blacklist bypass via obscure tags is a well-established class of vulnerability that recurs in custom sanitizers

knowledge.title: Plaintext tag destroys page rendering making XSS injection obvious
knowledge.knowledge: The <plaintext> HTML tag, while useful as a proof-of-concept for blacklist bypass, causes all subsequent page content to render as unformatted plain text. This makes the injection immediately visible to users and site operators, destroying operational stealth. Alternative obscure tags like <fn> and <credit> achieve the same blacklist bypass without disrupting page rendering, as they are parsed as inline elements with no default visual behavior.
trigger_condition: Discovering that <plaintext> passes through an HTML tag blacklist
Observing that injecting <plaintext> causes the rest of the page to render as raw HTML source text
Needing a stealthy persistent XSS payload that does not alert users or operators

specific: Any HTML context where <plaintext> is not blacklisted
general: Stored XSS scenarios requiring stealth
Wormable XSS development
Bug bounty demonstrations requiring clean proof-of-concept

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When selecting HTML tags for injection-based attacks, some tags that bypass filters have severe side effects on page rendering. Tags that alter the parsing mode of the document (plaintext, xmp, listing) will make the injection immediately obvious. Prefer tags with no default rendering behavior that still accept event handler attributes.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [a tag bypasses the filter] AND [that tag alters document parsing mode] THEN [the injection will be immediately visible and operationally unsuitable for persistent/stealthy XSS]
generalizes_to: choosing injection vectors that minimize detection

pitfalls: Reporting a <plaintext>-based XSS PoC may appear low-impact to triage teams because the visible page destruction seems to 'break' the attack — always provide an alternative clean tag in the report
The <xmp> and <listing> tags have similar page-destructive behavior to <plaintext> and should also be avoided for stealth
knowledge.chain_potential: Selecting a non-destructive tag enables the stored XSS to remain undetected longer, increasing the window for worm propagation or session theft
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Directly observed behavior — <plaintext> rendering behavior is defined in HTML spec and consistently implemented across browsers

knowledge.title: Stored profile XSS plus missing CSRF on profile edit enables self-propagating worm
knowledge.knowledge: On platforms where user profiles are publicly viewable and editable, the combination of stored XSS in a profile field and absent CSRF protection on the profile edit endpoint creates a self-propagating worm condition. The XSS payload on an attacker's profile can read the viewing user's identity from the DOM, construct a form POST to the profile edit endpoint (requiring no CSRF token), and inject the same payload into the victim's profile — causing exponential propagation as each newly-infected profile infects its viewers.
trigger_condition: Discovering stored XSS in a user profile field that is rendered when other users view the profile
Observing that the profile edit/save endpoint lacks CSRF token validation
Confirming that the DOM exposes the currently logged-in user's identity (e.g., username in a page element)

general: Social platforms with user-editable profiles
Marketplace platforms with seller pages
Forum or community platforms with profile bios
Any multi-user application with viewable and editable user-generated content

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a stored XSS exists in content that is viewed by other authenticated users, and the endpoint that stores that content lacks CSRF protection, the XSS can programmatically replicate itself into other users' content. This transforms a single stored XSS from an isolated compromise into a platform-wide worm. The severity multiplier is exponential rather than linear.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [stored XSS exists in user-viewable profile content] AND [profile edit endpoint has no CSRF protection] AND [logged-in user identity is readable from DOM or cookies] THEN [self-propagating XSS worm is achievable with exponential spread]
generalizes_to: XSS worm construction on social platforms
severity escalation of stored XSS findings
chaining low-severity findings into critical impact

pitfalls: If cookies are HTTPOnly, the worm can still propagate via CSRF-based replication even though direct cookie theft is prevented — do not dismiss the worm vector just because cookies are protected
Character limits on the profile field may require loading external scripts, which introduces a dependency on an attacker-controlled server remaining available
Wildcard cookie domains (*.ebay.com) expand the blast radius but are not strictly required for the worm to function on the same subdomain
knowledge.chain_potential: Combined with cookies stored on wildcard domain without HTTPOnly flag, the worm can simultaneously steal session cookies and propagate, enabling mass account takeover alongside viral spread
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Demonstrated end-to-end in a real-world eBay bug bounty finding; XSS worm patterns are historically validated (Samy worm on MySpace followed identical chain logic)

knowledge.title: Encoder/decoder asymmetry as vulnerability signal in protocol implementations
knowledge.knowledge: When a protocol library implements both encoding and decoding for the same field, the encoder may correctly enforce specification limits while the decoder does not. This asymmetry is a reliable indicator of exploitable parsing bugs because developers often implement the encoder first (or more carefully, since it handles trusted internal data) and treat the decoder as a simple mirror without applying the same boundary checks. In libcurl's MQTT implementation, mqtt_encode_len correctly limited Remaining Length to 4 bytes per MQTT v3.1.1 spec, while mqtt_decode_len had no such limit.
trigger_condition: Encountering a protocol library that implements both serialization and deserialization of the same structured field
Observing that the encoder/serializer contains explicit bounds checks or loop iteration limits
Discovering that the decoder/deserializer uses a data-driven loop termination (e.g., continuation bits) without an equivalent hard limit

general: Protocol parsers with encode/decode pairs
Binary protocol implementations
Any library implementing variable-length encoding schemes

abstracted_pattern.narrative: In protocol implementations, encoder and decoder functions for the same field are often written with different trust assumptions. The encoder handles internal trusted data and is written to produce spec-compliant output, while the decoder handles external untrusted data but paradoxically may lack the same validation. Diffing the constraints between the encode and decode paths for the same protocol field reveals missing validation in the decoder.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [encoder for field X enforces limit N] AND [decoder for field X uses data-driven loop termination without limit N] THEN [decoder accepts malformed input beyond spec limits, enabling protocol desynchronization or overflow]
generalizes_to: Variable-length integer decoding in any binary protocol
Protocol Buffers varint decoding in custom implementations

pitfalls: Assuming the decoder is correct because the encoder works properly — they may have been written at different times or by different developers
Only auditing the decoder in isolation without comparing it to the encoder's constraints, missing the asymmetry signal
Assuming that because the encoder limits to N bytes, the decoder must also — this needs explicit verification
knowledge.chain_potential: Decoder accepting excess bytes leads to protocol desynchronization, which can enable response injection or stream confusion attacks depending on the protocol's framing model
knowledge.confidence_rationale: This pattern is directly demonstrated in the libcurl MQTT code with exact encoder/decoder functions shown. The encode/decode asymmetry pattern is a well-established class of bugs across protocol implementations.

knowledge.title: Multiplier overflow to zero silently consumes input without affecting decoded length
knowledge.knowledge: In variable-length integer decoding schemes that use a running multiplier (mult *= base), on architectures where the multiplier variable can overflow to exactly zero, all subsequent input bytes are consumed and advance the read pointer but contribute nothing to the decoded value (since N * 0 = 0). This creates a situation where the decoded length is smaller than expected while the parser's position has advanced past 'hidden' bytes. On 32-bit systems with libcurl's MQTT decoder, mult (size_t) overflows to 0 after 5 multiplications by 128, meaning bytes 5+ of a Remaining Length field are consumed but invisible to the length calculation.
trigger_condition: Encountering a variable-length integer decoder using a running multiplier pattern (accumulator += (byte & mask) * mult; mult *= base)
Observing that the multiplier variable is a fixed-width integer type (especially 32-bit size_t or uint32_t)
Discovering that the loop has no iteration count limit independent of the continuation-bit check

specific: Any MQTT Remaining Length decoder on 32-bit
general: Variable-length quantity (VLQ) decoders
MIDI variable-length decoders on embedded systems
Custom varint implementations on constrained architectures

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a multiplier-based variable-length integer decoder runs on an architecture where the multiplier can overflow to zero, excess continuation bytes are silently consumed. The parser advances past these bytes (they are 'read') but the decoded integer does not reflect them. This desynchronizes the parser's stream position from the logical message boundaries, causing subsequent data to be misinterpreted.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [multiplier-based VLQ decoder] AND [no iteration limit] AND [multiplier overflows to zero at iteration N] THEN [bytes beyond iteration N are consumed but invisible to decoded value, causing stream position to desynchronize from logical framing]
generalizes_to: Protocol desynchronization via length field manipulation
Any variable-length encoding on memory-constrained architectures

pitfalls: Assuming integer overflow in the multiplier causes a crash or detectable error — overflow to zero is silent and the computation continues producing plausible-looking (but wrong) results
Testing only on 64-bit systems where the overflow requires far more iterations to trigger, making the bug appear unreachable
Focusing only on the decoded length value being wrong, while missing that the more exploitable consequence is the input pointer advancement past hidden bytes
knowledge.chain_potential: The desynchronization between decoded length and actual bytes consumed can cause payload bytes to be interpreted as packet headers, potentially enabling protocol-level injection of crafted MQTT control packets into the stream
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The arithmetic progression is deterministic and verified through the multiplication table shown in the report. The overflow-to-zero behavior of unsigned integer multiplication by powers of 2 is well-defined in C.

knowledge.title: Audit protocol spec mandatory limits as first pass in protocol parser review
knowledge.knowledge: Protocol specifications that use the word 'MUST' or 'MUST NOT' for field size limits (per RFC 2119) represent hard constraints that parsers are required to enforce. Searching for these mandatory limits in the spec and then checking whether the parser actually enforces them is a high-yield first-pass audit strategy. The MQTT v3.1.1 spec states 'The number of bytes in the Remaining Length field MUST NOT exceed four bytes' — libcurl's decoder did not enforce this. Specification-mandated limits that are missing from parsers create undefined protocol states that are exploitable for desynchronization.
trigger_condition: Encountering a library implementing a well-specified protocol (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/2, AMQP, etc.)
Observing that the protocol specification defines explicit MUST/MUST NOT constraints on field sizes or value ranges
Discovering variable-length or extensible fields in the protocol format that require parser-side enforcement

general: Any protocol parser implementing an RFC or formal specification
IoT protocol libraries
Binary protocol implementations in C/C++

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Mandatory specification limits (MUST/MUST NOT per RFC 2119) in protocol specifications are frequently unenforced in parser implementations, especially for variable-length fields where the natural loop termination condition (e.g., continuation bit not set) appears sufficient. The spec limit exists precisely because the natural termination is insufficient — violating it puts the parser into states the protocol designers explicitly prohibited.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [protocol spec defines MUST/MUST NOT constraint on field size] AND [parser uses data-driven termination for that field] AND [no explicit check enforces the spec limit] THEN [malformed input can push parser into undefined protocol state]

pitfalls: Assuming that data-driven loop termination (e.g., stop when continuation bit is clear) is equivalent to enforcing the spec limit — these are different constraints
Only checking the happy path where input happens to comply with the spec, missing that the parser must reject non-compliant input
Overlooking that 'entering an undefined protocol state' is exploitable even without memory corruption — desynchronization itself is impactful
knowledge.chain_potential: Undefined protocol states from spec violations can cascade into desynchronization, enabling packet injection, denial of service, or confusion attacks depending on how the implementation handles subsequent malformed framing
knowledge.confidence_rationale: This audit strategy is demonstrated directly in the report and generalizes well — missing MUST constraints are a recurring class of parser bugs across protocols.

knowledge.title: Server-side data fetch + content-type mismatch = reflected XSS via third-party profile
knowledge.knowledge: When an application scrapes or fetches data from an external source (e.g., a user's social media profile) and reflects it through an API endpoint that has an incorrect Content-Type header (text/html instead of application/json), the scraped content is rendered as HTML by browsers when the endpoint is visited directly. The payload resides in the third-party data source rather than in the request to the vulnerable endpoint, creating a stored-like XSS that is triggered via a simple GET request. Escaping performed client-side in the consuming JavaScript is irrelevant when the endpoint itself is browsed directly.
trigger_condition: Discovering an application feature that scrapes or imports data from an external URL or third-party profile
Observing that the AJAX/API endpoint returns Content-Type: text/html while the response body is actually JSON
Confirming the endpoint is accessible via direct GET request without CSRF tokens or session-bound restrictions

specific: Web applications using server-side scraping of social media profiles
general: Web applications with URL/page scraping features
API endpoints that import external content
Gallery/submission systems that auto-populate from external sources

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When an application fetches user-controlled data from a third-party source and reflects it through an endpoint with a misconfigured content-type, the combination creates an XSS where the attacker controls the payload indirectly. The attacker sets a malicious value in the source (e.g., their own profile description on another platform), and the vulnerable application fetches and serves it as HTML.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [API endpoint fetches data from attacker-controlled external source] AND [endpoint returns Content-Type: text/html while serving JSON/data] AND [endpoint is directly browsable without auth/CSRF] THEN [attacker can achieve XSS by poisoning their external profile data and directing victims to the raw endpoint]
generalizes_to: content-type mismatch exploitation on data aggregation endpoints

pitfalls: Focusing only on whether the client-side JavaScript escapes the data properly, missing that the raw endpoint is directly accessible and renders as HTML
Assuming that because the consuming page handles data safely, the underlying API endpoint is also safe
Overlooking the content-type header when the response looks like JSON — browsers respect content-type for rendering decisions
knowledge.chain_potential: The indirect payload delivery mechanism means the XSS payload is not visible in server access logs for the vulnerable endpoint, making detection harder. The external data source acts as a payload staging area.
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Verified in a real-world Facebook bug bounty finding with confirmed fix. The content-type mismatch pattern is a well-understood root cause, and the chain with external data sourcing is a concrete demonstrated technique.

knowledge.title: Browser XSS auditor bypass via server-side fetched payloads
knowledge.knowledge: Chrome's XSS Auditor and IE's XSS Filter operate by comparing the request parameters against the response body to detect reflected XSS. When the XSS payload is not present in the request (URL, body, or headers) but is instead fetched server-side from a third-party source and injected into the response, these client-side protections have no matching input to flag. The payload is effectively invisible to request-response correlation defenses because the malicious content enters the response through a server-side channel, not through the client's request.
trigger_condition: Encountering a reflected XSS scenario where the payload originates from server-side data fetching rather than direct user input in the request
Observing that the vulnerable endpoint pulls content from an external or database source that the attacker can influence indirectly
Confirming that the malicious string does not appear anywhere in the HTTP request to the vulnerable page

specific: Chrome XSS Auditor (deprecated in Chrome 78, but relevant for older targets)
general: Client-side XSS filters that use request-response correlation
WAFs that only inspect request parameters for XSS patterns
Security tools that match input to output for reflected XSS detection

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Any XSS defense mechanism that works by correlating the request content with the response content can be bypassed when the payload enters the response through a server-side channel (database lookup, third-party API call, file read) rather than through the attacker's direct request. The defense sees a clean request and fails to flag the malicious response.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [XSS defense relies on request-response payload matching] AND [payload is injected into response via server-side data source rather than request parameters] THEN [the defense is bypassed because the malicious content has no corresponding request-side signature]
generalizes_to: WAF bypass via indirect payload injection
server-side data channel abuse for filter evasion

pitfalls: Assuming this technique still works against Chrome's XSS Auditor — it was removed in Chrome 78 (2019), so this is primarily relevant for IE/legacy browsers or WAFs using similar correlation logic
Conflating this with stored XSS — the payload is not stored on the vulnerable application itself but on a third-party source, creating a hybrid between reflected and stored XSS
Forgetting that modern Content Security Policy (CSP) would still block inline script execution regardless of whether the auditor is bypassed
knowledge.chain_potential: Combines with content-type mismatch vulnerabilities to create XSS that evades both server-side content-type defenses and client-side auditor defenses simultaneously. Also useful when targeting environments with WAFs that inspect request bodies for XSS patterns.
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Demonstrated in a confirmed Facebook bug bounty report. The mechanism (request-response correlation bypass) is well-understood in the security community but the specific application via server-side scraping as a delivery channel is a non-obvious tactical insight.

knowledge.title: Conditional macro write with unconditional pointer adjust pattern
knowledge.knowledge: When a C macro or inline function conditionally writes to a buffer (guarded by a bounds check) but the calling code unconditionally adjusts the pointer afterward (e.g., `ptr -= 2` to 'undo' the writes), the pointer adjustment still executes even when the writes were skipped. This mismatch between conditional accumulation and unconditional correction creates a pointer underflow condition when the buffer is full. The pattern is especially insidious because the adjustment code appears correct when reading the happy path - it only fails when the guard condition suppresses the write.
trigger_condition: Encountering a macro or helper function that conditionally increments a buffer pointer only when space remains
Observing unconditional pointer arithmetic (decrement/subtraction) on the same pointer immediately after the conditional macro calls
Discovering a buffer size limit that can be reached through external input (e.g., protocol data filling a fixed-size buffer)

general: C/C++ codebases with macro-based buffer accumulation
Protocol parsers with fixed-size suboption buffers
Any code using conditional-write macros followed by pointer corrections

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When buffer write operations are conditional (guarded by bounds checks) but subsequent pointer adjustments assume the writes always occurred, filling the buffer to capacity causes the adjustments to underflow the pointer. The conditional path and the unconditional correction path become desynchronized at boundary conditions.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [macro/function conditionally writes to buffer and advances pointer] AND [calling code unconditionally decrements/adjusts the same pointer assuming writes occurred] THEN [filling the buffer to capacity causes pointer underflow by the adjustment amount]

pitfalls: The pattern is invisible during normal code review because the happy path (buffer not full) works correctly - the bug only manifests at the exact boundary condition
Macros hide the conditionality, making the calling code appear to have a straightforward write-then-adjust pattern
Static analyzers may not trace through macro expansions to detect the conditional/unconditional mismatch
knowledge.chain_potential: Pointer underflow lands on adjacent struct members, potentially enabling information disclosure of heap pointers or other sensitive fields that precede the buffer in the struct layout
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The pattern is directly demonstrated in the curl source code with clear root cause analysis; the conditional macro vs unconditional adjustment mismatch is a verifiable code pattern

knowledge.title: Struct-adjacent field leak via intra-struct pointer underflow
knowledge.knowledge: When a pointer underflows within a C struct (pointing before the buffer's start but still within the struct), the read lands on whatever struct fields are declared before the buffer. In structs containing pointer-rich types (like dynamic buffer descriptors with heap addresses) adjacent to fixed-size protocol buffers, an underflow of even 2 bytes can reach into those fields. The subsequent use of the corrupted pointer in length-calculating macros (e.g., `end - start` where start is now before the buffer) produces large wrapped/negative lengths, causing read functions to dump adjacent memory contents. When verbose/debug output is enabled, this leaked data is written to logs or stderr.
trigger_condition: Discovering a pointer underflow primitive within a struct containing both fixed-size buffers and pointer/heap-referencing fields
Observing that the struct layout places sensitive fields (heap pointers, dynamic buffer descriptors) immediately before the vulnerable fixed-size buffer
Discovering that a length-calculation macro uses pointer subtraction on the corrupted pointers, and the result feeds into a read/print function

specific: curl TELNET struct with dynbuf out adjacent to subbuffer[512]
general: C protocol handlers with struct-embedded fixed buffers
Network protocol implementations with debug/verbose output modes
Any C struct where pointer-containing metadata is adjacent to data buffers

abstracted_pattern.narrative: A buffer pointer underflow within a struct turns a buffer-related bug into an information disclosure of struct fields that precede the buffer in memory. When those fields contain heap pointers or other sensitive metadata, and the application has a verbose/debug output path that renders the buffer contents, the leaked data becomes visible to the attacker. The severity multiplies when the leaked pointers can be used for ASLR bypass in a chained exploit.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [pointer underflow occurs within a struct-embedded buffer] AND [struct fields preceding the buffer contain heap pointers or sensitive data] AND [a read/print function consumes the corrupted pointer range] THEN [adjacent struct field contents are disclosed, potentially leaking heap layout information]

pitfalls: The leaked data is only visible when verbose/debug mode is active, so the vulnerability may appear unexploitable in default configurations
Struct field ordering can vary between compiler versions and optimization levels, making the exact fields reached by underflow platform-dependent
ASLR bypass value of leaked pointers depends on the target having additional exploitable vulnerabilities to chain with
knowledge.chain_potential: Leaked heap pointers from the struct field disclosure can defeat ASLR, enabling exploitation of separate memory corruption vulnerabilities that require known addresses
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The struct layout and underflow mechanics are verified in curl source, but the practical exploitability of the leak depends on verbose mode being enabled and on specific compiler struct layout decisions

knowledge.title: Inconsistent control character rejection across protocol handler parsing functions indicates injection surfaces
knowledge.knowledge: In libcurl's SMTP handler, the `smtp_parse_address` function (handling CURLOPT_MAIL_FROM, CURLOPT_MAIL_RCPT, CURLOPT_MAIL_AUTH) lacks the REJECT_CTRL validation that is present in other SMTP input paths such as custom request handling. When a library applies control character filtering in some protocol parsing paths but not others, the unprotected paths are injection surfaces. The 'suffix' component parsed by smtp_parse_address is concatenated directly into raw protocol commands via Curl_pp_sendf without sanitization.
trigger_condition: Observing that a protocol client library has multiple input parsing functions for the same protocol (e.g., separate parsers for address fields vs. custom commands)
Discovering that one parsing path applies control character rejection (like REJECT_CTRL or equivalent) while another does not
Confirming that the unvalidated output is concatenated into raw protocol command strings

general: Protocol client libraries with multiple input parsing paths
Libraries using shared formatting functions (like pingpong sendf) with inconsistent input validation

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a protocol client library has multiple entry points that feed into the same protocol command construction, inconsistent input validation across those entry points creates injection surfaces. The presence of validation in one path serves as evidence that the developers recognized the risk, making unvalidated paths likely oversights rather than intentional design.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [a protocol library validates control characters in one parsing function] AND [a sibling parsing function for the same protocol lacks equivalent validation] THEN [the unvalidated function is a CRLF/command injection surface]

pitfalls: Assuming that because one input path is validated, all paths must be — the inconsistency IS the vulnerability
Only checking the obvious input fields (like custom commands) and missing structured fields (like email address components) that are decomposed into sub-parts before concatenation
Missing that the 'suffix' portion of a parsed address (everything after the email in angle brackets) may be passed through unfiltered even when the core address is validated
knowledge.chain_potential: The CRLF injection enables protocol desynchronization when combined with missing overflow detection in response handlers, escalating from injection to full command smuggling with misattributed responses
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Directly demonstrated in the report with code-level evidence from libcurl source; the inconsistency pattern between REJECT_CTRL usage across functions is a concrete, verifiable observation

knowledge.title: CRLF injection + missing pingpong overflow check enables protocol desynchronization with misattributed responses
knowledge.knowledge: In libcurl's SMTP implementation, the pingpong mechanism (lib/pingpong.c) sends one command and expects one response. When CRLF injection causes multiple commands to be sent in a single write, the server returns multiple responses. The STARTTLS handler correctly checks the `pp.overflow` flag to detect extra buffered responses, but the MAIL, RCPT, and DATA response handlers (at lines ~1152, ~1173, ~1233 in smtp.c) do not check this flag. This means injected command responses remain in the buffer and are consumed as responses to subsequent legitimate commands, causing curl to report success or failure based on responses to smuggled commands rather than the actual commands it intended to send.
trigger_condition: Discovering a request-response protocol client that uses a sequential 'send one, read one' state machine pattern (pingpong-style)
Observing that some state handlers check for extra/overflow buffered data while others do not
Confirming that an injection point exists that allows multiple protocol commands to be sent in a single transmission

general: Protocol client libraries using sequential request-response state machines
FTP/SMTP/POP3/IMAP clients with line-based command-response patterns
Any pingpong-style protocol handler

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a protocol client uses a strict one-command-one-response state machine and an attacker can inject additional commands, the server's extra responses accumulate in the receive buffer. If the client does not verify that no extra data exists after reading an expected response, subsequent state transitions consume the stale buffered responses instead of the actual server replies to legitimate commands. This desynchronizes the client's understanding of the protocol state from reality, allowing the attacker to control what the client believes succeeded or failed.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [a protocol client uses sequential send-one-read-one state machine] AND [CRLF injection allows multiple commands in one send] AND [response handlers do not check for extra buffered data] THEN [client-server protocol state becomes desynchronized and the client misattributes responses to wrong commands]

pitfalls: Assuming CRLF injection alone is the full impact — without the missing overflow check, the client would likely error on unexpected responses rather than silently misattribute them
Testing only with simple single-command injection and missing the desynchronization effect that requires observing multi-step protocol interaction
Overlooking that libcurl uses the same pingpong mechanism for FTP, POP3, and IMAP — similar overflow check inconsistencies may exist in those protocol handlers

Crafting injection payloads that produce response codes mismatched with what the state machine expects, causing an error instead of silent desynchronization — the attacker must align injected response codes with the expected state transitions
knowledge.chain_potential: The desynchronization enables silent email fraud where curl reports successful delivery to legitimate recipients while actually having delivered to smuggled recipients, and can be leveraged as an SSRF vector to tunnel commands into internal SMTP relays
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The report provides specific code line references showing the presence of pp.overflow checking in STARTTLS and its absence in MAIL/RCPT/DATA handlers, with a working PoC demonstrating the desynchronization

knowledge.title: When auditing pingpong protocol handlers, diff the overflow/extra-data checks across all state handlers
knowledge.knowledge: In libcurl's pingpong-based protocol handling, the presence of a `pp.overflow` check in the STARTTLS handler but not in MAIL/RCPT/DATA handlers reveals that the defensive pattern was known to the developers but inconsistently applied. When auditing any protocol client library that uses a shared response-reading mechanism, the most efficient approach is to identify all state handler functions and diff which ones implement extra-data/overflow detection versus which ones skip it. The handlers that skip the check are the highest-priority injection targets because they allow silent desynchronization rather than error-causing failures.
trigger_condition: Encountering a protocol client library that uses a shared request-response reading mechanism (like libcurl's pingpong) across multiple protocol states
Discovering that at least one state handler implements an overflow or extra-data safety check
Observing that the protocol accepts user-controlled input that flows into command construction

specific: libcurl lib/pingpong.c and all protocol handlers using it (SMTP, FTP, POP3, IMAP)
general: Protocol client libraries with shared response-parsing infrastructure
Any client using sequential state machines for text-based protocols

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a security check exists in one handler of a shared protocol mechanism but is absent in sibling handlers, the inconsistency itself is the signal. The existing check proves the developers knew about the risk class, and every handler missing that check is a candidate vulnerability. Systematically diffing all handlers against the one with the check is far more efficient than blind fuzzing.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [a shared protocol mechanism has a safety check in one state handler] AND [sibling state handlers lack the same check] THEN [the unchecked handlers are high-priority targets for the exact attack class the check was meant to prevent]

pitfalls: Assuming the overflow check was added proactively rather than reactively — it may have been added for STARTTLS specifically due to TLS upgrade sensitivity and not considered for other states
Only checking the same protocol (SMTP) and missing that libcurl's pingpong mechanism is shared across FTP, POP3, and IMAP, which may have similar inconsistencies
Focusing only on the overflow flag and missing other inconsistent safety patterns in the same codebase
knowledge.chain_potential: Identifying all unprotected pingpong state handlers across libcurl's supported protocols could reveal similar desynchronization vulnerabilities in FTP, POP3, and IMAP handlers
knowledge.confidence_rationale: The specific inconsistency between STARTTLS and MAIL/RCPT/DATA handlers is documented with exact line numbers in the report, and the audit methodology of diffing safety checks across sibling handlers is a proven technique

knowledge.title: Inconsistent format specifier usage as over-read signal
knowledge.knowledge: When a codebase correctly uses precision-limited format specifiers (e.g., %.*s) for non-null-terminated buffers in one code path but uses unbounded %s in another code path within the same file, the inconsistent path is likely a buffer over-read vulnerability. In curl's lib/http2.c, the trailer-handling code correctly used %.*s with length parameters from nghttp2, while the PUSH_PROMISE handling used bare %s on the same type of non-null-terminated buffer pointers, indicating the PUSH_PROMISE path was written or reviewed with less rigor.
trigger_condition: Observing a C/C++ codebase that processes callback data from a library providing non-null-terminated buffers with explicit length parameters
Discovering that some code paths in the same file use precision specifiers (%.*s) while others use bare %s on the same buffer type
Confirming that the inconsistent code path handles a less common or default-disabled feature

general: C libraries processing protocol callbacks
HTTP/2 implementations
Any code using printf-family functions on length-delimited buffers

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Within a single source file handling multiple subtypes of the same event callback, if the common subtypes correctly apply bounds-checked formatting but a rarer subtype does not, the rarer subtype likely has a buffer over-read. Developers often get the pattern right for the primary code path but miss it on secondary paths that share the same raw pointer contract.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [same callback provides non-null-terminated buffers] AND [common frame type handler uses %.*s correctly] AND [rare frame type handler uses %s on same buffer type] THEN [rare path has heap buffer over-read]

pitfalls: Assuming that if one code path in a file handles buffers correctly, all paths do - developers copy-paste patterns inconsistently
Only auditing the most common frame type handlers and skipping rarely-triggered ones like PUSH_PROMISE
Missing the vulnerability because the inconsistency is separated by hundreds of lines in the same file
knowledge.chain_potential: The over-read can leak adjacent heap data into header strings that may be logged or forwarded, enabling information disclosure from a client application's memory
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Verified in curl source code; the fix is straightforward and the inconsistency is directly observable by diffing format specifier usage across frame type handlers in the same file

knowledge.title: Default-disabled protocol features as high-value audit targets
knowledge.knowledge: Protocol features that are disabled by default in the most common client interface (e.g., HTTP/2 Server Push is disabled in curl CLI) but enabled in library usage (libcurl) tend to have less battle-tested code paths. These paths receive minimal fuzzing and real-world exercise, making them disproportionately likely to contain memory safety bugs. In curl, the PUSH_PROMISE handling code had a format string vulnerability that survived in the same file where the heavily-exercised trailer path was correctly implemented.
trigger_condition: Encountering a protocol library that exposes features disabled by default in its primary CLI or reference client
Observing that the feature is still available and enabled in the library API used by third-party applications
Discovering that the default-disabled feature's code path handles the same types of untrusted input as the well-tested paths

general: Protocol libraries with CLI wrappers
HTTP/2 implementations
Any library where default configuration differs between CLI and API usage

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a protocol implementation's primary interface disables a feature by default, the code implementing that feature receives dramatically less real-world testing. If the library API still allows enabling it, applications using that API inherit undertested code paths that may contain vulnerabilities absent from the well-exercised default paths.
