abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [feature disabled by default in primary client] AND [feature available via library API] AND [feature handles same untrusted input types as tested paths] THEN [feature's code path is disproportionately likely to contain bugs]

pitfalls: Dismissing default-disabled features as unexploitable without checking if downstream library consumers enable them
Assuming the feature is entirely dead code when it may be activated by server-initiated negotiation or library-level configuration
Focusing only on features visible in CLI help output rather than auditing the full library API surface
knowledge.chain_potential: Identifying undertested paths provides a focused attack surface for finding memory corruption, which can chain with information disclosure or denial of service depending on how the calling application handles the corrupted data
knowledge.confidence_rationale: This pattern is well-demonstrated by the curl PUSH_PROMISE case and generalizes to many protocol libraries where CLI and library exposure diverge; similar patterns have appeared in OpenSSL optional extensions and other network libraries

knowledge.title: Server-initiated frame + non-null-terminated callback buffer = client over-read
knowledge.knowledge: In HTTP/2, PUSH_PROMISE is a server-initiated frame that causes the client to process attacker-controlled header names and values via the nghttp2 on_header callback. The callback provides raw pointers with separate length fields (not null-terminated). When client code concatenates these into strings using printf-family functions without precision specifiers, a malicious server can trigger heap over-reads on the client by sending PUSH_PROMISE frames with crafted headers. The over-read size is unbounded (reads until a null byte in heap), potentially causing OOM crashes or leaking heap contents into the constructed header string.
trigger_condition: Encountering an HTTP/2 client implementation that accepts server push (PUSH_PROMISE)
Observing that the client processes pushed headers through a callback providing non-null-terminated buffers
Discovering that the header assembly code uses unbounded string operations (sprintf %s, strcpy, strcat) on these buffers

general: HTTP/2 client implementations
Protocol clients processing server-initiated frames
Callback-based protocol parsing libraries in C/C++

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Server-initiated protocol frames (push promises, server-sent events, unsolicited notifications) give attackers a vector to inject data into client-side processing paths. When these paths use a callback API that provides length-delimited (non-null-terminated) buffers, and the consuming code treats them as C strings, the server can trigger unbounded heap reads on the client.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [server can initiate frames containing attacker-controlled data] AND [client callback receives non-null-terminated buffers with length] AND [client code uses C string operations without length bounds] THEN [malicious server triggers heap over-read on client]

pitfalls: Assuming HTTP/2 push is never enabled - many library-level consumers (web browsers, API clients) may enable it
Testing only with well-behaved servers that happen to send null-terminated data, masking the bug
Overlooking that the over-read data may end up in logs, error messages, or forwarded headers, creating a secondary information disclosure channel
knowledge.chain_potential: The over-read string is stored as a header value, which may be logged by the application (leaking heap data to log files), forwarded to upstream services (leaking data cross-service), or cause OOM when the over-read spans megabytes of heap until finding a null byte
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Directly verified in curl source code with ASAN-confirmed reproduction; the nghttp2 documentation explicitly states buffers are not null-terminated, making this a documented API contract violation

knowledge.title: Vendor-patched XSS regression via payload variant in CMS settings APIs
knowledge.knowledge: When a CMS vendor claims to have patched a stored XSS vulnerability in a settings API endpoint, the fix is often payload-specific rather than implementing proper output encoding. The original proof-of-concept payload may be blocked while variant payloads (e.g., different script block closure/injection sequences) remain effective. In Ghost Blog, the XSS patched in v0.5.9 was reachable again from at least v1.24.9 through v2.2.0 because the remediation addressed the specific payload rather than the root cause of unsanitized rendering of settings values into page templates.
trigger_condition: Encountering a CMS or web application that previously disclosed and 'patched' a stored XSS in its settings or configuration API
Observing that the original PoC payload from the prior disclosure no longer triggers execution
Discovering that the settings values (logo URLs, header/footer injection fields, image paths) are still rendered directly into HTML templates without context-aware output encoding

general: Node.js CMS platforms
Blog platforms with theme/template rendering
Any CMS with admin-configurable site-wide settings rendered into HTML

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a content management system patches a stored XSS by blocking a specific payload pattern rather than implementing proper context-aware output encoding at the template rendering layer, the vulnerability is likely to be re-exploitable with payload variants. The fix addresses symptoms rather than the root cause, making regression inevitable across version upgrades or when new features touch the same rendering path.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [CMS has prior XSS disclosure in settings/config endpoint] AND [original PoC payload is blocked but values still rendered into HTML without encoding] THEN [variant payloads closing/reopening script or tag contexts are likely to succeed]
generalizes_to: patch validation for output encoding vulnerabilities
settings API security in template-rendering platforms

pitfalls: Assuming a vendor's patch actually addressed the root cause rather than just blocking the specific reported payload
Stopping testing after confirming the original PoC no longer works, rather than trying variant payloads

Overlooking that template rendering contexts differ between fields - logo may render in an img src attribute context while ghost_head renders in a raw script context, requiring different payload variants
knowledge.chain_potential: A regression XSS in site-wide settings means every page served by the CMS executes the payload, enabling credential harvesting, session hijacking of other admins, or watering-hole attacks against all site visitors if an admin account is compromised through any means
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Confirmed in one specific product (Ghost) across multiple versions, but the pattern of payload-specific XSS patches rather than root-cause fixes is commonly observed across CMS platforms

knowledge.title: Partial JSON body in PUT settings APIs accepts subset of fields
knowledge.knowledge: CMS settings APIs that accept PUT requests with JSON bodies often do not require the complete settings object to be submitted. Sending only the targeted parameter (e.g., a single field like 'logo' or 'cover_image') with a malicious value is sufficient for the server to process and persist the change. This means an attacker does not need to know or reconstruct the full settings schema to exploit injection points in any individual field.
trigger_condition: Encountering a CMS or application settings API that accepts PUT requests with a JSON body containing multiple configuration fields
Observing that the API returns a large settings object with many fields on GET requests
Discovering that the API does not enforce schema validation requiring all fields to be present in PUT/PATCH requests

general: RESTful CMS settings APIs
JSON-based configuration endpoints
Node.js Express-based API frameworks with permissive body parsing

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Configuration or settings endpoints in web applications frequently accept partial updates even when the HTTP method is PUT (which semantically implies full resource replacement). This permissive behavior reduces the prerequisite knowledge an attacker needs - instead of reconstructing the entire settings schema, they can target individual fields for injection. This is especially useful when combined with stored XSS or injection into fields that are rendered application-wide.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [settings API accepts PUT with JSON body] AND [multiple fields exist in the settings object] THEN [submitting only the targeted field with a malicious value is likely sufficient for persistence]
generalizes_to: stored injection via partial resource updates
REST API semantic method handling weaknesses

pitfalls: Assuming PUT strictly requires the full resource representation - many implementations treat PUT like PATCH for backwards compatibility or developer convenience
Failing to test individual fields in isolation, which may reveal that some fields have stricter validation than others
Sending all fields when only one is needed, which may trigger validation errors on unrelated fields and mask the actual vulnerability
knowledge.chain_potential: Reduces exploitation complexity for stored XSS or injection attacks by eliminating the need to enumerate and correctly populate all settings fields, making automated exploitation more reliable
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Directly confirmed in Ghost Blog, and consistent with common REST API implementation patterns where frameworks like Express merge partial JSON bodies into existing objects

knowledge.title: First-visit-only parameters as hidden XSS attack surface
knowledge.knowledge: Web applications that display one-time UI elements to new visitors (age verification gates, compliance popups, onboarding flows, cookie consent dialogs) frequently inject additional URL parameters that are only present during that first interaction. These parameters are under-tested because most bug hunters browse a site in an authenticated/returning-user state before beginning testing, meaning these parameters never appear in their workflow. The attack surface of these ephemeral parameters is disproportionately likely to contain reflected XSS because they receive less scrutiny from both internal QA and external researchers.
trigger_condition: Encountering a web application that has a first-visit gate, age verification popup, compliance dialog, or onboarding flow before main content access
Observing that the URL changes or gains new parameters after interacting with the first-visit element (e.g., a compliancePop, gateway, or welcome parameter appears)
Discovering that these parameters are absent when revisiting the site in the same browser session

specific: Adult content platforms with age verification
general: Any web application with first-visit-only UI flows
Sites with age/compliance/GDPR gates
SaaS onboarding wizards
E-commerce first-visit discount popups

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Applications that conditionally inject URL parameters based on session state (first visit, unauthenticated, specific user segment) create attack surface that is invisible to testers who have already established a session. Testing from a fresh/incognito state reveals parameters and input vectors that are hidden from returning visitors, and these parameters tend to be less hardened because they receive less testing attention.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [application shows one-time UI elements to new visitors] AND [URL parameters change after interacting with those elements] THEN [those ephemeral parameters are disproportionately likely to be vulnerable to injection because they are undertested]
generalizes_to: attack surface that varies by user state or segment
any feature gated behind a one-time interaction

pitfalls: Forgetting to clear cookies/use incognito for each test iteration, causing the first-visit flow to not trigger again
Assuming that because the main application input fields are well-sanitized, ancillary one-time parameters receive the same sanitization pipeline
Only testing the visible form inputs on the gate page while ignoring URL parameters that were silently added during the redirect
knowledge.chain_potential: First-visit parameters with reflected XSS can be weaponized against all new visitors since victims by definition have not yet established a session - making phishing links more credible as the victim expects to see a gate/verification page
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Demonstrated in one public report on a major platform; the pattern is logical and consistent with the general principle that less-tested code paths have more bugs, but limited to a single confirmed instance in this writeup

knowledge.title: Fragment-routed AJAX + unanchored regex domain check = CORS-based XSS
knowledge.knowledge: When a single-page application uses URL fragment routing to load pages via AJAX, and the domain validation regex is not anchored to the start of the string (e.g., /m\.flickr\.com/ instead of /^https?:\/\/m\.flickr\.com/), the validation can be satisfied by placing the target domain string anywhere in an attacker-controlled URL (such as a query parameter value). Combined with CORS headers on the attacker's server and the application inserting AJAX responses via innerHTML, this creates a full XSS chain where the attacker's external content is fetched cross-origin and rendered in the target application's DOM context.
trigger_condition: Observing a single-page application that loads page content via AJAX based on URL fragment values
Discovering a domain validation regex in client-side JavaScript that lacks start-of-string anchoring (^ anchor)
Confirming the AJAX response body is inserted into the DOM via innerHTML or similar unsanitized method

general: Single-page applications with fragment-based routing
Client-side URL validation in AJAX loaders
Pre-pushState SPAs using hash routing

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When an application validates that a URL belongs to a trusted domain using a regex that is not anchored to the beginning of the string, an attacker can satisfy the check by embedding the trusted domain string elsewhere in a URL pointing to attacker-controlled infrastructure. If the application then fetches that URL cross-origin (enabled by CORS) and renders the response unsafely, the attacker achieves code execution in the application's origin.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [client-side URL validation uses unanchored domain regex] AND [validated URL is fetched via AJAX] AND [response is inserted via innerHTML] THEN [attacker can serve malicious content from external domain that includes the trusted domain string as a parameter value, achieving XSS]

pitfalls: Assuming a domain regex provides security without checking for ^ anchor — many developers visually verify 'does it check the domain' without noticing the missing anchor
Forgetting that the attacker's server must return proper CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Headers) for the cross-origin AJAX request to succeed
Missing that the leading-slash prepend logic can be bypassed with protocol-relative URLs (//attacker.com) — a double slash passes a 'starts with slash' check while being a valid cross-origin URL
Overlooking that additional Access-Control-Allow-Headers may be needed to prevent JavaScript parsing errors in the application's AJAX handler
knowledge.chain_potential: The innerHTML injection enables full XSS, which can be chained into session hijacking, CSRF token theft, or API abuse within the authenticated session on the target origin
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Verified in a real-world bug bounty finding on Flickr (Yahoo), with complete exploit chain demonstrated and fix confirmed

knowledge.title: Satisfying unanchored domain regex via query parameter embedding
knowledge.knowledge: An unanchored domain validation regex (one missing the ^ start anchor) can be satisfied by placing the required domain string as a query parameter value in an attacker-controlled URL. For example, a regex checking for 'm.flickr.com' anywhere in a URL is satisfied by '//attacker.com/page?bypass=m.flickr.com'. This works because regex engines search the entire string by default. The technique is especially effective when combined with protocol-relative URLs (//) that also bypass 'starts with slash' checks while resolving to the attacker's server.
trigger_condition: Discovering a client-side regex that validates a URL against a domain pattern without ^ anchoring
Observing that the code also checks for a leading slash character but does not distinguish single slash from double slash (protocol-relative URL)
Confirming the validated URL is subsequently used in a fetch/AJAX call rather than just navigation

general: Any client-side or server-side URL allowlist using unanchored regex
URL validation in redirect parameters
Domain validation in postMessage origin checks
OAuth redirect_uri validation

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a system validates that a URL contains a trusted domain string using regex without anchoring to the start, the trusted string can be embedded as a query parameter, path segment, or subdomain of an attacker-controlled URL to pass the check while the URL still resolves to the attacker's server.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [URL validation regex lacks ^ anchor] AND [attacker controls the full URL structure] THEN [trusted domain string can be placed in query parameters or path while URL resolves to attacker infrastructure]

pitfalls: The dot character in domain regex (e.g., m.flickr.com) often is not escaped, meaning 'mXflickrYcom' would also match — but exploiting this is rarely needed when the query parameter technique works
Some implementations may parse the URL before regex matching, extracting just the hostname — in that case this bypass fails and a different approach is needed
Protocol-relative URLs (//) may be blocked by some URL parsers or CSP policies, requiring alternative bypass approaches
knowledge.chain_potential: Enables loading attacker-controlled content in the context of the target application, which can be chained with DOM-based injection sinks (innerHTML, document.write, eval) for XSS
knowledge.confidence_rationale: This is a well-established regex bypass pattern verified across multiple bug bounty reports, and the specific query-parameter embedding technique is a proven reliable method

knowledge.title: Fragment-based AJAX routing signals potential CORS-assisted content injection
knowledge.knowledge: When a web application uses URL fragment (hash) routing to determine which content to load via AJAX (common in pre-pushState SPAs), the AJAX loading function typically contains URL validation logic that may be bypassable. The presence of fragment-based routing combined with AJAX content loading is a strong signal that the application may be vulnerable to content injection if the URL validation has flaws, because: (1) the fragment value is fully attacker-controlled via crafted links, (2) AJAX requests can be made cross-origin with CORS, and (3) AJAX responses are commonly inserted into the DOM unsafely. This pattern is distinct from modern pushState routing which typically uses server-side routing and is less likely to have client-side-only URL validation.
trigger_condition: Observing a web application where page navigation updates location.hash and triggers AJAX content loading
Discovering that the JavaScript reads location.hash and passes it to an AJAX fetch function
Noticing client-side URL validation (regex or string checks) in the AJAX loading path rather than server-side enforcement

general: Single-page applications with fragment routing
Legacy mobile web applications
Any application loading content via AJAX based on user-controlled URL input

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Applications that use client-controlled URL fragments to determine AJAX content sources are inherently trusting client input for content origin decisions. When the validation of that input occurs only in client-side JavaScript (which the attacker can study), any flaw in the validation creates a direct path to content injection in the application's DOM.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [application uses fragment-based AJAX content loading] AND [URL validation is client-side only] THEN [validation logic is fully visible to attacker and any bypass leads to cross-origin content injection]

pitfalls: Modern SPAs using pushState may still have similar patterns but with different URL handling — the absence of fragment routing does not mean the application is safe from similar issues
Some fragment-routed applications use server-side proxies for AJAX requests, which would prevent direct CORS-based exploitation
The CORS preflight (OPTIONS request) may fail if the attacker's server is not properly configured, causing the exploit to silently fail without obvious error
knowledge.chain_potential: Identifying this pattern focuses subsequent testing on the specific URL validation logic, which if bypassed enables serving attacker content into the application's DOM for XSS
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Fragment-based AJAX routing is increasingly rare in modern applications, but the pattern remains relevant for legacy applications and the underlying principle transfers to modern client-side routing

knowledge.title: XSS to RCE chain in Electron-based desktop apps via Node.js bridge
knowledge.knowledge: Desktop applications built on Electron (or similar Node.js-based frameworks) that render user-controlled content have an implicit bridge between DOM-level XSS and operating system-level code execution. When the renderer process has Node.js integration enabled, standard browser-context XSS payloads gain access to Node.js APIs such as `process.open`, `require('child_process').exec`, and filesystem operations. The XSS impact jumps from session-level to system-level without any additional exploit or privilege escalation being required.
trigger_condition: Encountering a desktop application that renders user-supplied content (usernames, chat messages, display names) in its UI
Observing that the application is built on Electron, NW.js, or another Node.js-backed desktop framework (visible in binary inspection, process listing, or shipped node_modules)
Confirming that Node.js integration is enabled in the renderer context (process object is accessible from injected JavaScript)

general: Desktop applications with embedded web renderers and native code bridges
Hybrid desktop applications
Chat and conferencing clients

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When a desktop application uses a web rendering engine with a bridge to native/system APIs, any XSS vulnerability in that renderer context implicitly becomes a remote code execution vulnerability. The severity of XSS findings must be re-evaluated based on the execution context—browser-context XSS in a desktop app with native bridges is fundamentally different from browser-context XSS in an actual browser.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [XSS exists in user-controlled content rendered by a desktop app] AND [the rendering engine has Node.js integration or native API bridge enabled] THEN [XSS is equivalent to RCE on every user who renders that content]
generalizes_to: Any hybrid app with web-to-native bridges (React Native with native modules, Cordova with plugins)
WebView-based mobile apps with JavaScript bridges

pitfalls: Assuming Electron apps have nodeIntegration disabled by default—older versions had it enabled by default, and many apps still explicitly enable it or use preload scripts that expose equivalent functionality
Reporting this as just XSS without demonstrating the RCE chain, which drastically undervalues the finding's severity
Overlooking contextIsolation settings—even with nodeIntegration disabled, if contextIsolation is off, prototype pollution can reach the Node.js context
knowledge.chain_potential: In multi-user applications (conferencing, chat), this chain becomes wormable—one user's XSS payload executes on every other participant's machine, enabling mass RCE without individual targeting
knowledge.confidence_rationale: This is a well-demonstrated pattern confirmed across multiple real-world Electron app vulnerabilities (conferencing apps, Slack, VS Code extensions, etc.)

knowledge.title: eval(prompt()) as interactive XSS debug console
knowledge.knowledge: The payload `` at only 35 characters provides an interactive JavaScript execution console within a live XSS context. Each time the payload triggers, a prompt dialog appears where arbitrary JavaScript can be typed and executed immediately in the vulnerable page's context. This is significantly more efficient for impact exploration than encoding each test payload via String.fromCharCode or other filter-bypass encoding, especially when character limits or quote filtering exist.
trigger_condition: Discovering a reflected or stored XSS with character-length constraints or input filtering (e.g., quote stripping)
Observing that the XSS fires in a context where interactive dialog boxes (prompt/alert) are functional
Needing to rapidly test multiple follow-on payloads to assess impact depth without re-injecting each time

specific: Stored XSS in web applications with input length limits
general: Any XSS exploitation scenario requiring iterative payload development
Constrained injection points where payload iteration cost is high

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When exploring the impact of an XSS vulnerability under constrained conditions (character limits, filtering), injecting a minimal interactive execution primitive (eval + prompt) is more efficient than crafting complete encoded payloads for each test. The one-time cost of a small bootstrap payload amortizes across many rapid iterations of impact assessment.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [XSS is confirmed but payload space is constrained or filtering exists] AND [interactive dialogs are functional in the target context] THEN [a minimal eval(prompt()) bootstrap payload enables rapid iterative exploration at far lower cost per test than individually crafted payloads]
generalizes_to: Bootstrapping larger payloads from minimal injection points

pitfalls: This only works when the XSS fires in a context where the user (attacker) can interact with the prompt dialog—it is useless for blind/stored XSS targeting other users where you need a non-interactive payload
Some modern browsers and Electron configurations suppress or block prompt() dialogs in certain contexts
The approach requires the XSS to fire while the tester is actively viewing the page—timing-dependent stored XSS may not cooperate
knowledge.chain_potential: Enables rapid discovery of available APIs in the execution context (e.g., testing for process.open, require, filesystem access) which determines the ultimate severity of the chain
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Directly demonstrated in this engagement and is a reusable technique applicable to any interactive XSS assessment

knowledge.title: Unauthenticated multi-user contexts as XSS severity multipliers
knowledge.knowledge: In applications where sessions are unauthenticated or use easily guessable/public identifiers (e.g., conferencing meeting IDs, public chat rooms, shared documents), stored XSS in user-controlled display fields (names, avatars, status messages) has a multiplicative blast radius. Every participant who joins the shared context renders the attacker's payload. The combination of unauthenticated entry plus stored XSS in a shared rendering context transforms a single injection into a mass-targeting vector without requiring any social engineering per-victim.
trigger_condition: Encountering a multi-user application feature where joining does not require per-user authentication (public meeting links, open chat rooms, shared workspaces with link-only access)
Observing that user-controlled display data (display names, profile fields) is rendered to all other participants in the shared context
Discovering that input validation on these display fields is performed client-side only or is inconsistent between web and native clients

general: Any multi-user application with unauthenticated or low-authentication shared contexts
Applications where user-supplied display data is rendered to other participants

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When an application allows unauthenticated or low-barrier entry into shared contexts where user-controlled data is rendered to all participants, the blast radius of any stored injection vulnerability scales with the number of participants rather than requiring individual targeting. The severity assessment must account for the authentication model of the shared context, not just the injection itself.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [shared context allows unauthenticated or link-only joining] AND [user-controlled display data is rendered to all participants] AND [injection is possible in display fields] THEN [a single injection targets all current and future participants without per-victim effort]
generalizes_to: Mass exploitation via shared unauthenticated contexts
Severity assessment for injection vulnerabilities in multi-tenant shared spaces

pitfalls: Assuming that brute-forcing the meeting/room ID space is necessary—many meeting links are shared publicly on calendars, social media, or support pages
Underestimating severity because the initial XSS payload seems benign—the multiplication effect across all participants makes even information-stealing XSS high severity
Forgetting that validation differences between web and native clients may mean the native client accepts payloads the web client blocks (bypassing length limits or filtering)
knowledge.chain_potential: Combined with the Electron/Node.js RCE chain, this creates a wormable RCE vector: join any public meeting → inject display name → achieve RCE on all participants' machines
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Directly demonstrated in this report and the pattern is broadly applicable to the entire class of collaborative applications

knowledge.title: $.getScript as compact XSS payload stager bypassing quote filters
knowledge.knowledge: When jQuery is loaded on the target page and quote characters are filtered, `$.getScript(String.fromCharCode(...))` serves as an extremely compact external script loader (the entire bootstrap payload fits in ~80 characters). Unlike constructing inline payloads that must work around character filtering and length limits, this technique offloads all complexity to an attacker-controlled external script. The jQuery `$.getScript` function both fetches and executes the remote JavaScript in the page's context, including any Node.js/native bridges available.
trigger_condition: Observing that the target application loads jQuery ($ or jQuery object available in global scope)
Encountering quote character filtering or stripping in the injection point that prevents inline string construction
Operating under a character length constraint that prohibits complex inline payloads

general: Any XSS scenario with character constraints where a JavaScript library with script-loading capabilities is already present
Staged payload delivery through existing library functions

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When an injection point has both character constraints and character filtering, leveraging an already-loaded JavaScript library's remote script loading capability converts the constrained injection into an unconstrained external payload. The injection point only needs to bootstrap a loader, not contain the full exploit.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [XSS injection point has character limits and quote filtering] AND [a JavaScript library with remote script loading is already loaded on the page] THEN [the injection can be reduced to a minimal loader call with String.fromCharCode encoding, offloading all payload complexity to an external server]
generalizes_to: Leveraging existing page libraries for exploitation
Bypassing input length restrictions via external resource loading

pitfalls: Content Security Policy (CSP) with script-src restrictions will block the external script fetch—this technique fails silently if CSP is enforced
Assuming jQuery is the only option—other libraries like Angular (ng-include), Prototype, or even native fetch/import() may serve the same purpose
The external hosting domain must serve the script with appropriate CORS headers or be same-protocol, and short domains (like xor.cc) help fit within character limits
knowledge.chain_potential: Enables loading arbitrarily complex second-stage payloads including reverse shells, credential harvesters, or in the Electron context, full system compromise via Node.js APIs
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Well-established technique demonstrated in this report and widely applicable wherever jQuery is present

knowledge.title: Cross-parameter tag splitting to bypass per-parameter XSS filters
knowledge.knowledge: When a server-side XSS filter checks for the co-occurrence of '<' followed by '>' within the same parameter value (e.g., matching <.*>), the filter is defeatable if two parameters are reflected adjacently in the same HTML/script context. The HTML parser's tag-matching behavior is tolerant of intervening characters between a closing tag's slash and its final '>', meaning '</script/','dest':">' still functions as a valid closing </script> tag from the HTML parser's perspective, even though the ',' '"', ':' characters appear between '</script/' and '>'.
trigger_condition: Observing that a reflected parameter encodes or strips content only when both '<' and '>' appear together in the same parameter value
Discovering that two or more parameters are reflected adjacently within the same inline <script> block or HTML context
Confirming that '<' alone and '>' alone pass through the filter unencoded in separate parameters

general: Web applications with per-parameter XSS sanitization
Multi-parameter reflection in inline script contexts
Applications using regex-based HTML tag detection

abstracted_pattern.narrative: When input sanitization operates independently on each parameter but multiple parameters are rendered contiguously in the same execution context, payloads can be split across parameters to avoid triggering per-parameter pattern detection. The HTML parser reconstructs a valid tag from fragments that span across non-tag content because HTML parsing is more permissive than the sanitizer's regex assumes.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [per-parameter sanitizer blocks co-occurrence of open+close angle brackets] AND [multiple parameters reflected adjacently in same context] THEN [splitting tag opener into one parameter and closer into another bypasses the filter because HTML parsers tolerate intervening characters in malformed closing tags]
generalizes_to: any context where sanitization boundary differs from parser boundary

pitfalls: The intervening characters between the two parameters must not break the HTML parser's ability to match the closing tag — test with the specific rendering template to confirm
Browser XSS auditors (e.g., Chrome's now-removed XSS Auditor) may still block the payload even if the server-side filter is bypassed, so testing in multiple browsers is necessary
The </script/> alternate closing syntax is HTML-parser specific and does not work in all contexts — it works for closing script tags but may not generalize to other tags
knowledge.chain_potential: Once script context is escaped, arbitrary JavaScript execution enables session theft, especially if httpOnly cookies are leaked into JS variables
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Verified in a real-world bug bounty finding on a production application, with working proof of concept demonstrated

knowledge.title: Inconsistent encoding across parameters signals separate code paths with different security controls
knowledge.knowledge: When different URL parameters reflected on the same page exhibit different encoding behaviors (e.g., some encode double quotes as %22 while others encode as \", or some encode angle brackets while others do not), this indicates the parameters are processed by different server-side code paths with different sanitization logic. Parameters with weaker or different encoding are likely handled by a less mature or separately maintained code path and are higher-priority targets for injection.
trigger_condition: Observing that the same special character (e.g., ", <, >) is encoded differently across different parameters reflected on the same page
Confirming that at least two parameters appear in the same execution context (e.g., same inline script block) despite having different encoding

specific: Tokopedia train ticket search parameters (ori/dest vs adult/infant/trip)
general: Web applications with URL path parameters mixed with query parameters
Applications where routing parameters and form parameters are handled by different middleware
Monolithic applications with multiple internal services rendering to one page

abstracted_pattern.narrative: Encoding inconsistency across co-rendered parameters is a reliable signal that different code paths handle different parameters. Differential encoding means differential security controls, and the weakest code path determines the exploitability of the combined rendering context.
abstracted_pattern.pattern: IF [multiple parameters rendered in same context show different encoding behavior for the same character] AND [parameters share an execution context] THEN [at least one code path has weaker sanitization and is the primary exploitation target]

pitfalls: The encoding difference might be intentional and both paths might be secure — differential encoding alone does not confirm vulnerability, only that different code paths exist
Testing only one parameter and assuming all parameters share the same filter will cause you to miss the vulnerability entirely
knowledge.chain_potential: Identifying the weakly-sanitized parameter enables targeted payload crafting, especially when combined with cross-parameter payload splitting
knowledge.confidence_rationale: Directly observed pattern that led to a validated vulnerability; encoding inconsistency as a signal for differing code paths is a well-established testing heuristic confirmed here
